title: Your First Observation: Nature Journaling in the Field
description: Begin your nature journaling practice with a structured sit spot session. You will observe a single outdoor location for 30 minutes using all your senses and record what you find.

steps.instruction: Find a spot outdoors where you can sit comfortably for 15-20 minutes. This can be a backyard, park, schoolyard, or any semi-natural area. Read about outdoor observation -- what is the 'sit spot' practice and why does returning to the same spot over time reveal things a single visit cannot?
steps.expectedOutcome: You understand why patient, repeated observation of a single location reveals patterns invisible to casual passersby
steps.hint: Even an ordinary urban park has surprising wildlife if you sit quietly and wait -- the animals are usually there, just hiding from your movement and noise

steps.instruction: Open your nature journal (or any notebook). Record: date, time, location (as specific as possible), weather (temperature feel, cloud cover, wind direction), and a brief sketch of your surroundings. Read about nature journaling -- what does a complete journal entry look like?
steps.expectedOutcome: You have begun a journal entry with the minimum required documentation for a scientific nature observation
steps.hint: Sketch the view from where you sit -- it does not need to be beautiful, just spatially accurate enough to orient a future reader

steps.instruction: Sit quietly for 10 minutes without writing. Use all your senses: What do you hear? What do you smell? What is moving? When wildlife returns to normal activity around you, note: date-stamped observations of specific organisms. What plants, insects, birds, or other animals do you detect?
steps.expectedOutcome: You have at least 3-5 specific organism observations with time, behavior, and your best identification attempt
steps.hint: Close your eyes and listen for 2 minutes -- you will hear birds you were not noticing while focused on sight

steps.instruction: Choose one organism you observed and sketch it in detail with labels. Read about tree identification or bird identification -- can you identify what you sketched? Use field marks (size, shape, color patterns, habitat). Note what you are uncertain about.
steps.expectedOutcome: You have made an illustrated species observation with your best identification and honest uncertainty notes
steps.hint: It is more scientifically valuable to note 'I am not sure' than to guess -- uncertainty is information

steps.instruction: Write 3 'I wonder...' questions that arose during your observation. Then consider how to submit your best observation to iNaturalist. Read about citizen science -- how do platforms like iNaturalist use observations like yours?
steps.expectedOutcome: You have formulated genuine inquiry questions and understand how citizen science platforms aggregate individual observations into research-grade data
